title: Tension Pneumothorax
clinicalContext: A 24-year-old male is brought to the emergency department after a motorcycle accident. He has a penetrating chest wound on the right side. He is tachypneic at 32 breaths/min, tachycardic at 128 bpm, and hypotensive with BP 78/50 mmHg. Breath sounds are absent on the right, and the trachea appears deviated to the left. Neck veins are distended.
learningObjectives: Understand the anatomy of the pleural space and its role in normal respiration
Explain the pathophysiology of the one-way valve mechanism in tension pneumothorax
Recognize the classic clinical presentation and physical exam findings
Understand the hemodynamic consequences of tension physiology
Apply evidence-based management including needle decompression technique
Differentiate tension pneumothorax from other causes of obstructive shock

guidelineReferences.name: StatPearls: Tension Pneumothorax
guidelineReferences.citation: Sahota RJ, Sayad E. Tension Pneumothorax. StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025. PMID: 31082072

guidelineReferences.name: BTS Pleural Disease Guideline 2023
guidelineReferences.citation: Roberts ME, Rahman NM, Maskell NA, et al. British Thoracic Society Guideline for pleural disease. Thorax. 2023;78(Suppl 3):s1-s42. PMID: 37553157

guidelineReferences.name: ATLS Needle Decompression Meta-Analysis 2025
guidelineReferences.citation: Ahmad R, et al. Meta-analysis of the optimal needle length and decompression site for tension pneumothorax. World J Emerg Surg. 2025;20:39. PMC12087068

layers.name: Anatomy & Structure
layers.description: Understanding pleural anatomy and the structural basis for pneumothorax

questions.question: The pleural space normally contains approximately how much fluid, and what is its primary function?
questions.answer: The pleural space normally contains 10-20 mL of serous fluid that serves as a lubricant to reduce friction during breathing and creates an adhesive force that couples the lung to the chest wall.

whyChain.why: Why is this small amount of fluid so important?
whyChain.answer: The pleural fluid creates surface tension that keeps the visceral pleura (lung surface) adherent to the parietal pleura (chest wall), allowing the lung to expand when the chest wall expands during inspiration.
whyChain.source: OpenStax A&P 2e, Chapter 22.3 - The Process of Breathing

whyChain.why: Why does this coupling matter for breathing mechanics?
whyChain.answer: Normal intrapleural pressure is negative (-4 to -8 cmH2O) relative to atmospheric pressure. This negative pressure, maintained by the elastic recoil of the lung pulling inward and the chest wall pulling outward, keeps the lungs inflated.
whyChain.source: OpenStax A&P 2e, Chapter 22.3 - The Process of Breathing

questions.question: Where are the two anatomical sites recommended for needle decompression, and what are the landmark structures?
questions.answer: The two sites are: (1) 2nd intercostal space at the midclavicular line (traditional ATLS), and (2) 4th-5th intercostal space at the anterior axillary or midaxillary line (current ATLS 10th edition preference). Needles are inserted just above the rib to avoid the neurovascular bundle.

whyChain.why: Why is the lateral site now preferred in ATLS guidelines?
whyChain.answer: Studies show chest wall thickness at the 5th ICS midaxillary line is significantly less than at the 2nd ICS midclavicular line, leading to higher success rates with standard-length needles, especially in obese patients.
whyChain.source: ATLS Needle Decompression Meta-Analysis. World J Emerg Surg. 2025

whyChain.why: Why might the anterior site still be used?
whyChain.answer: For left-sided pneumothorax, the 2nd ICS midclavicular line is safer because the lateral approach carries higher risk of cardiac injury. Also, in moving ambulances, the anterior site provides better stabilization.
whyChain.source: ATLS Needle Decompression Meta-Analysis. World J Emerg Surg. 2025

layers.name: Pathophysiology
layers.description: Understanding the mechanism of tension physiology

questions.question: What is the 'one-way valve' mechanism that creates tension pneumothorax?
questions.answer: A one-way valve forms when damaged tissue allows air to enter the pleural space during inspiration (when negative pressure draws air in) but prevents air from escaping during expiration (when the tissue flap closes). This causes progressive air accumulation with each breath.

whyChain.why: Why does this lead to hemodynamic collapse?
whyChain.answer: Progressive air accumulation increases intrathoracic pressure, which compresses the ipsilateral lung (causing hypoxia), shifts the mediastinum to the contralateral side (compressing the opposite lung), and compresses the vena cava (reducing venous return to the heart).

whyChain.why: Why is reduced venous return so dangerous?
whyChain.answer: Decreased venous return leads to reduced cardiac preload, which decreases cardiac output via the Frank-Starling mechanism. This causes obstructive shock - a form of shock where mechanical obstruction prevents adequate cardiac filling.

questions.question: Why are patients on positive pressure ventilation at particularly high risk for tension pneumothorax?
questions.answer: Positive pressure ventilation (PPV) actively forces air into the lungs. If there's any breach in the pleura, PPV drives air into the pleural space under pressure, accelerating the development of tension physiology much faster than would occur with spontaneous breathing.

whyChain.why: Why should PPV be avoided before chest decompression in suspected tension pneumothorax?
whyChain.answer: PPV can rapidly convert a simple pneumothorax into a tension pneumothorax or worsen existing tension physiology by forcing more air through the one-way valve into the pleural space.

questions.question: What are the causes of tension pneumothorax?
questions.answer: Causes include: (1) Penetrating chest trauma, (2) Blunt chest trauma with rib fractures, (3) Barotrauma from mechanical ventilation, (4) Iatrogenic (central line placement, thoracentesis, lung biopsy), (5) Progression of simple pneumothorax, (6) Failed occlusive dressing on open chest wound.

whyChain.why: Why can an occlusive dressing cause tension pneumothorax?
whyChain.answer: An improperly applied occlusive dressing (taped on all four sides) on an open chest wound can act as a one-way valve - air enters through the wound during inspiration but cannot escape during expiration. A three-sided dressing allows air to escape.

questions.question: What is the classic triad of physical findings in tension pneumothorax?
questions.answer: The classic triad includes: (1) Hypotension (from decreased cardiac output), (2) Absent/decreased breath sounds on the affected side, and (3) Distended neck veins (JVD) from impaired venous return. Tracheal deviation away from the affected side is a late and inconsistent finding.

whyChain.why: Why is tracheal deviation considered a late finding?
whyChain.answer: Tracheal deviation requires significant mediastinal shift, which only occurs after substantial air accumulation. By the time tracheal deviation is visible, the patient may be in extremis. Earlier signs include tachycardia, tachypnea, and hypoxia.

whyChain.why: Why might JVD be absent despite tension pneumothorax?
whyChain.answer: JVD may be absent in patients who are also hypovolemic (e.g., trauma patients with hemorrhage). The combination of reduced venous return from tension AND reduced circulating volume can result in flat neck veins despite elevated intrathoracic pressure.

questions.question: What percussion finding is expected in tension pneumothorax, and what is the underlying mechanism?
questions.answer: Hyperresonance (tympany) is expected over the affected hemithorax. This occurs because the pleural space is filled with air rather than lung tissue. Air-filled spaces transmit sound waves differently than solid tissue, producing a drum-like quality.

whyChain.why: Why might hyperresonance be difficult to appreciate in a noisy trauma bay?
whyChain.answer: Percussion requires a quiet environment to appreciate subtle differences in sound. In loud trauma settings, other clinical signs (absent breath sounds, hypotension, JVD) may be more reliable indicators.

questions.question: Why is tension pneumothorax a clinical diagnosis that should NOT wait for imaging confirmation?
questions.answer: Tension pneumothorax can progress to cardiac arrest within minutes. Waiting for chest X-ray delays life-saving intervention. If a patient is hemodynamically unstable with clinical signs suggestive of tension pneumothorax, immediate needle decompression is indicated.

whyChain.why: What if needle decompression is performed and there's no tension pneumothorax?
whyChain.answer: The risk of iatrogenic simple pneumothorax from needle decompression is acceptable given the lethal consequences of untreated tension pneumothorax. A small iatrogenic pneumothorax can be managed with observation or chest tube.

questions.question: What are the chest X-ray findings of tension pneumothorax?
questions.answer: CXR findings include: (1) Absence of lung markings on affected side, (2) Mediastinal shift to contralateral side, (3) Flattening or inversion of ipsilateral hemidiaphragm, (4) Widened intercostal spaces on affected side, (5) Deep sulcus sign (in supine patients).

whyChain.why: Why is the diaphragm flattened or inverted?
whyChain.answer: Increased pressure in the pleural space pushes the diaphragm downward. In severe cases, the diaphragm may actually invert (curve downward instead of upward), indicating high intrathoracic pressure.

questions.question: How can bedside ultrasound (POCUS) rapidly diagnose pneumothorax?
questions.answer: Ultrasound findings include: (1) Absence of lung sliding (normal lung slides against chest wall with breathing), (2) Absence of B-lines (normal vertical artifacts from lung), (3) Presence of A-lines only (horizontal artifacts from air), (4) 'Barcode sign' on M-mode (stratosphere sign) instead of normal 'seashore sign'.

whyChain.why: Why is ultrasound faster than X-ray for diagnosing pneumothorax?
whyChain.answer: POCUS can be performed at the bedside in seconds during resuscitation without interrupting care. It has higher sensitivity than supine chest X-ray for detecting pneumothorax (sensitivity ~90% vs ~50% for supine CXR).

questions.question: Describe the technique for needle decompression of tension pneumothorax.
questions.answer: Insert a 14-16 gauge angiocatheter (≥8 cm length for adults) perpendicular to the chest wall, just above the rib (to avoid neurovascular bundle), at either the 2nd ICS midclavicular line or 4th-5th ICS anterior axillary line. A rush of air confirms placement. Leave catheter in place until chest tube is inserted.

whyChain.why: Why is an 8 cm or longer needle recommended?
whyChain.answer: Studies show that chest wall thickness exceeds 4.5 cm in >50% of adults at the 2nd ICS midclavicular line. Standard 5 cm catheters fail to reach the pleural space in many patients. Longer catheters (≥8 cm) have significantly higher success rates.
whyChain.source: ATLS Needle Decompression Meta-Analysis. World J Emerg Surg. 2025

whyChain.why: What if needle decompression fails?
whyChain.answer: If no air rush occurs or patient doesn't improve, consider: (1) needle too short, (2) wrong location, (3) catheter kinked/occluded, (4) alternative diagnosis. Proceed to finger thoracostomy or chest tube. Don't delay definitive treatment.

questions.question: What is the definitive treatment after needle decompression?
questions.answer: Tube thoracostomy (chest tube insertion) is the definitive treatment. Typically a 28-32 Fr chest tube is inserted in the 4th-5th intercostal space at the anterior or midaxillary line and connected to an underwater seal drainage system or one-way valve (Heimlich valve).

whyChain.why: Why is a chest tube needed after successful needle decompression?
whyChain.answer: Needle decompression converts tension pneumothorax to simple pneumothorax but doesn't evacuate all the air or prevent re-accumulation. The catheter can become kinked or dislodged. A chest tube provides continuous drainage and lung re-expansion.

questions.question: What is finger thoracostomy, and when is it indicated?
questions.answer: Finger thoracostomy is a rapid surgical approach where a small incision is made in the 4th-5th ICS anterior axillary line, followed by blunt dissection into the pleural space and finger sweep to confirm entry. It's indicated when needle decompression fails or in cardiac arrest with suspected tension pneumothorax.

whyChain.why: Why might finger thoracostomy be preferred in cardiac arrest?
whyChain.answer: In cardiac arrest, time is critical. Finger thoracostomy is faster and more reliable than needle decompression, eliminates concerns about needle length or catheter kinking, and allows immediate insertion of a chest tube.

layers.name: Differential Diagnosis
layers.description: Distinguishing tension pneumothorax from other causes of shock

questions.question: How do you differentiate tension pneumothorax from cardiac tamponade?
questions.answer: Both cause obstructive shock with JVD and hypotension. Key differences: Tension pneumothorax has absent breath sounds on one side and hyperresonance; cardiac tamponade has muffled heart sounds and no respiratory findings. FAST ultrasound shows pericardial effusion in tamponade vs. absent lung sliding in pneumothorax.

whyChain.why: Why is this differentiation clinically important?
whyChain.answer: The treatments are different and delay can be fatal. Tension pneumothorax requires needle decompression/chest tube; cardiac tamponade requires pericardiocentesis. Wrong treatment could worsen the patient.

questions.question: What other conditions should be considered in the differential diagnosis of tension pneumothorax?
questions.answer: Differential includes: (1) Massive hemothorax (dullness to percussion, not hyperresonance), (2) Massive pulmonary embolism (usually no unilateral findings), (3) Right ventricular MI (ECG changes), (4) Severe asthma/COPD exacerbation (bilateral wheezing), (5) Flail chest (paradoxical chest movement).

whyChain.why: Why is hemothorax important to distinguish from pneumothorax?
whyChain.answer: Both cause absent breath sounds but hemothorax produces dullness to percussion (fluid) while pneumothorax produces hyperresonance (air). Hemothorax requires blood transfusion and possible surgical intervention rather than simple decompression.

questions.question: A trauma patient in cardiac arrest has signs suggesting tension pneumothorax. What is the recommended approach?
questions.answer: Bilateral finger thoracostomies should be performed as part of resuscitation (even without confirmation) because: (1) Tension pneumothorax is a reversible cause of PEA arrest, (2) Clinical diagnosis is unreliable in arrest, (3) The procedure is fast and the risk-benefit ratio favors intervention, (4) Bilateral approach ensures the correct side is decompressed.

whyChain.why: Why bilateral and not just the suspected side?
whyChain.answer: In cardiac arrest, you cannot reliably assess breath sounds or other lateralizing signs. Time is critical, and missing a tension pneumothorax on either side is lethal. Bilateral thoracostomies take minimal extra time and ensure complete decompression.

questions.question: How do you apply the principles of tension pneumothorax management to our case patient?
questions.answer: The 24-year-old with penetrating chest trauma, absent right breath sounds, tracheal deviation left, hypotension, and JVD has classic tension pneumothorax. Immediate management: (1) High-flow oxygen, (2) Needle decompression or finger thoracostomy on the RIGHT (not waiting for X-ray), (3) Prepare for tube thoracostomy, (4) Reassess hemodynamics after decompression.

whyChain.why: What if the patient doesn't improve after decompression?
whyChain.answer: Consider: (1) Procedure failure - repeat decompression or escalate to thoracostomy, (2) Concurrent pathology - hemothorax, cardiac tamponade, (3) Other sources of shock - hemorrhage from other injuries. FAST exam and re-evaluation are critical.
